text: Every write API returns a journal receipt: imports stage into a WAL directory and the only way chunks become visible is a single atomic commitEntry(receipt) rename, so a half-finished import is a directory you can delete, never a corrupt vault.
rationale: The 3am page for 'vault corrupted after app killed mid-import' becomes impossible when partial state is structurally quarantined and crash recovery is 'rm the staging dir', diagnosable by ls alone.

text: The unlock session is a non-Sendable, non-copyable ~Copyable struct whose deinit zeroes the DEK — the type system makes 'session that never dies' and 'DEK escapes to another thread' compile errors, not runtime audits.
rationale: Leaked-plaintext and immortal-session pages come from reference-typed sessions stashed in singletons; ownership-consumed handles mean the lifetime bug can't be written, so the on-call engineer never hunts for who's still holding the key.

text: Reads never return plain Data: they return a TaggedPlaintext wrapper that carries the chunk address, AEAD-verification proof, and byte range it satisfies, so any code path serving unverified or misaligned bytes fails to typecheck.
rationale: The scariest page is 'video player showed garbage / tampered chunk got through'; making verification a type-level precondition of possessing plaintext means the audit is grep-for-Data instead of tracing every read path.

text: Ship a first-class fsck: VaultCore exposes verify(gallery:) that walks manifest→chunks→AEAD tags without the password (ciphertext hashes only) and emits a machine-readable damage report distinguishing 'missing chunk', 'bad tag', 'orphan blob'.
rationale: At 3am you don't want to reason about the CAS invariants in your head — you want one command that tells you exactly which invariant broke, runnable on a locked vault pulled off a user's device, which also becomes the CLI peer's first tool for free.

text: Every failable operation errors with a persisted incident breadcrumb: errors are enum cases carrying (galleryID, fileID, chunkIndex, expectedHash, gotHash) and VaultCore appends them to an encrypted incident log inside the vault itself.
rationale: The difference between a 5-minute and 5-hour page is whether the error says 'decryptFailed' or 'chunk 41 of file X failed tag check, hash mismatch abc≠def'; baking forensics into the error surface means the report IS the diagnosis.

text: Concurrency ownership is inverted: VaultCore is entirely synchronous, single-threaded, and blocking — one serialized VaultActor at the boundary owns all mutation, and callers get async only via that one choke point, so there is exactly one interleaving to reason about at 3am.
rationale: Race-condition pages ('two imports interleaved and HEAD points at a manifest referencing missing chunks') are unreproducible nightmares; a deliberately boring single-writer core trades peak parallelism for a state machine you can replay in your head.
